content.text: A user simulator plays the role of the human in a multi-turn agent evaluation. Instead of paying human annotators to type messages into an agent during testing, you run a program that sends messages, reads the agent's replies, and decides what to say next — all automatically. User simulators make multi-turn evaluation scalable: a run that would take a human annotator an hour to drive by hand can execute in seconds at CI speed. The two fundamental types are scripted simulators, which follow a fixed message sequence, and LLM-powered simulators, which generate messages dynamically in response to the agent.

attrs.alt: Scripted vs LLM-powered user simulator: scripted is deterministic and cheap; LLM-powered is realistic and discovers novel flows

content.text: A scripted simulator sends a pre-authored sequence of messages regardless of what the agent replies. This is the right choice for regression testing: you want to run the exact same conversation every time and detect if the agent's responses change. Scripted simulators cost nothing to run (no LLM call for the user turn), are fully reproducible, and make test failures easy to diagnose.

An LLM-powered simulator generates each user message dynamically by calling a language model with a persona description, the user's goal, and the conversation so far. Because the simulator reacts to what the agent said, it discovers conversation paths that a static script would never exercise: the agent's unexpected response causes the simulated user to ask a follow-up question that wasn't anticipated.

from anthropic import Anthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

client = Anthropic()

@dataclass
class UserPersona:
    name: str
    description: str     # who this user is
    goal: str            # what they want to accomplish
    style: str           # how they communicate (terse, verbose, frustrated, polite)

SIMULATOR_SYSTEM = """You are roleplaying as a user in a conversation with an AI assistant.

Persona: {description}
Goal: {goal}
Communication style: {style}

Rules:
- Stay in character. Do not break the fourth wall.
- Pursue your goal naturally. Ask follow-ups if the agent is unclear.
- Reply with ONLY the next user message — no stage directions, no labels.
- Once your goal is fully achieved, reply with exactly: GOAL_ACHIEVED
- If the agent is unable to help after 3 attempts, reply with exactly: GIVE_UP
"""

class LLMUserSimulator:
    def __init__(
        self,
        persona: UserPersona,
        model: str = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_turns: int = 10,
    ):
        self.persona = persona
        self.model = model
        self.max_turns = max_turns
        self.history: list[dict] = []
        self._done = False
        self._goal_achieved = False

    def _system(self) -> str:
        return SIMULATOR_SYSTEM.format(
            description=self.persona.description,
            goal=self.persona.goal,
            style=self.persona.style,
        )

    def next_message(self) -> str | None:
        """Generate the next user message, or None if the conversation is over."""
        if self._done or len(self.history) >= self.max_turns * 2:
            return None

        msg = client.messages.create(
            model=self.model,
            max_tokens=256,
            system=self._system(),
            messages=self.history,
        )
        text = msg.content[0].text.strip()

        if text == "GOAL_ACHIEVED":
            self._done = True
            self._goal_achieved = True
            return None
        if text == "GIVE_UP":
            self._done = True
            return None

        self.history.append({"role": "user", "content": text})
        return text

    def record_agent_reply(self, reply: str) -> None:
        self.history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": reply})

    @property
    def goal_achieved(self) -> bool:
        return self._goal_achieved

# Example personas
FRUSTRATED_CUSTOMER = UserPersona(
    name="frustrated_customer",
    description="A customer who received a damaged product and is frustrated after already contacting support once before without resolution.",
    goal="Get a full refund for order ORD-123 and a confirmation email.",
    style="Terse, impatient, slightly annoyed. Uses short sentences. Quick to escalate if not helped.",
)

TECH_SAVVY_USER = UserPersona(
    name="tech_savvy",
    description="A software developer comfortable with technical language who wants precise information.",
    goal="Understand the exact API rate limits for the enterprise tier and how burst traffic is handled.",
    style="Direct, uses technical terminology, asks specific follow-up questions about edge cases.",
)

NOVICE_USER = UserPersona(
    name="novice",
    description="A non-technical user who has never used the product's API before.",
    goal="Set up a basic API integration for the first time.",
    style="Asks basic questions, easily confused by jargon, needs step-by-step guidance.",
)

The eval harness drives a conversation by alternating between the user simulator and the agent under test. It captures the full transcript and records when the conversation ended and why. The harness works identically for scripted and LLM-powered simulators since both expose the same interface.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class ConversationRecord:
    case_id: str
    persona_name: str
    turns: list[dict]          # [{role, content}]
    goal_achieved: bool
    turn_count: int
    termination_reason: str    # "goal", "max_turns", "gave_up", "early_stop"

def run_conversation(
    case_id: str,
    simulator,             # ScriptedSimulator or LLMUserSimulator
    agent_fn,             # (messages: list[dict]) -> str
    max_turns: int = 12,
) -> ConversationRecord:
    """Drive a full multi-turn conversation between simulator and agent."""
    messages: list[dict] = []
    termination_reason = "max_turns"

    for _ in range(max_turns):
        # User turn
        if hasattr(simulator, "has_next"):
            # Scripted simulator
            if not simulator.has_next():
                termination_reason = "script_exhausted"
                break
            user_msg = simulator.next_message()
        else:
            # LLM simulator
            user_msg = simulator.next_message()
            if user_msg is None:
                termination_reason = "goal" if simulator.goal_achieved else "gave_up"
                break

        messages.append({"role": "user", "content": user_msg})

        # Agent turn
        agent_reply = agent_fn(messages)
        messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": agent_reply})

        # Let simulator react to the agent reply
        if hasattr(simulator, "record_agent_reply"):
            simulator.record_agent_reply(agent_reply)

        # Scripted: check for early stop
        if hasattr(simulator, "_stopped_early") and simulator._stopped_early:
            termination_reason = "early_stop"
            break

    # Determine goal achievement
    if hasattr(simulator, "goal_achieved"):
        goal_achieved = simulator.goal_achieved
    else:
        goal_achieved = getattr(simulator, "completed_fully", False)

    persona_name = getattr(getattr(simulator, "persona", None), "name", "scripted")

    return ConversationRecord(
        case_id=case_id,
        persona_name=persona_name,
        turns=messages,
        goal_achieved=goal_achieved,
        turn_count=len(messages) // 2,
        termination_reason=termination_reason,
    )

A persona library is a curated set of user simulators covering the range of users your agent will encounter in production. A good persona library covers the happy path, frustrated users, edge-case communicators, and adversarial inputs. Run every eval case against multiple personas to find which user types the agent handles poorly.

For scripted simulators, success is mechanical: did the script complete without early stop? For LLM simulators, the GOAL_ACHIEVED sentinel signals the model's self-reported success, which may not be reliable. Back this up with a separate LLM judge that reads the transcript and scores whether the user's stated goal was met independently of what the simulator reported.

User simulators replace human annotators in multi-turn agent evaluation. Scripted simulators replay fixed message sequences for deterministic regression testing at zero cost. LLM-powered simulators generate messages dynamically in response to the agent, discovering conversation paths a static script would miss. A persona library spanning different user types reveals which user populations the agent handles poorly. Back the simulator's self-reported goal achievement with a separate LLM judge to avoid over-counting successes where the simulator gave up prematurely. Running persona sweeps in CI gives continuous visibility into multi-turn quality without any manual annotation.
